Course Title: IoT + AI: Expanding the Attack Surface
Category: Technology & Telecommunications: Information Technology, Science & Technology, Software, Hardware, Telecom
Description: Connected devices using AI in uncontrolled environments.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how the internet works and a general curiosity about connected devices and security concepts is all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key security vulnerabilities introduced by AI-enabled IoT devices and explain how connected technologies expand the attack surface in real-world environments. You will also apply foundational risk assessment techniques to recognise and prioritise threats posed by AI-driven devices operating in uncontrolled or public-facing settings.

lessons.lesson title: IoT + AI: Expanding the Attack Surface
lessons.Description: The rapid growth of Internet of Things (IoT) devices, combined with the increasing use of artificial intelligence, has created a significantly larger and more complex attack surface for organisations to manage. Connected devices such as smart sensors, cameras, and industrial controllers are often deployed in uncontrolled or remote environments, making them difficult to monitor and secure effectively. When AI is layered on top of these devices, new vulnerabilities can emerge that traditional security approaches are not always equipped to handle. This lesson introduces the key risks associated with IoT and AI working together, using clear and practical examples to help beginners understand where threats can arise. By the end of this lesson, you will have a foundational understanding of why securing connected, AI-enabled devices requires a fresh and considered approach to cybersecurity.
lessons.content: Connected devices using AI in uncontrolled environments.

slides.slidetitle: IoT and AI: The Expanding Attack Landscape
slides.subtitle: Where connectivity meets vulnerability
slides.slidecontent:
• Billions of devices now connected to the internet
• AI makes devices autonomous and adaptive
• More functionality equals more attack vectors
• Criminals target everyday objects, not just computers
• Security often treated as an afterthought in product design

slides.subtitle: Real-world consequences of virtual vulnerabilities
slides.slidecontent:
• IoT devices bridge digital networks and physical systems
• Vulnerabilities can cause tangible harm beyond data breaches
• AI adds unpredictable behaviors even developers didn't anticipate
• Medical devices, safety systems, and infrastructure at risk
• Many devices never receive security updates after deployment

slides.subtitle: When anyone can walk up and interfere
slides.slidecontent:
• Devices mounted in corridors, car parks, and public areas
• No protective housing or tamper-evident seals
• Physical access enables device tampering, rebooting, or theft
• Exposed USB ports and reset buttons invite interference
• AI cameras and sensors installed without considering physical security

slides.subtitle: Securing devices in uncontrolled environments
slides.slidecontent:
• Field sensors and meters deployed without physical supervision
• No guards, cameras, or monitoring of remote installations
• Attackers can extract firmware and credentials from unguarded hardware
• Small form factors make devices easy to steal or swap unnoticed
• Physical compromise bypasses all software security controls

slides.subtitle: Why proper physical safeguards get skipped
slides.slidecontent:
• Protective enclosures and tamper detection add deployment costs
• Debug interfaces and service ports left accessible to save time
• Device labels reveal model numbers and firmware versions to attackers
• Brief physical access sufficient for credential extraction or hardware insertion
• Stolen devices hand over local AI models and all stored data

slides.subtitle: When devices advertise their own vulnerabilities
slides.slidecontent:
• Model numbers and firmware versions printed on device exteriors
• Attackers can research known vulnerabilities before attempting access
• Service documentation often publicly available online
• Default configurations and backdoor accounts easily identified
• Physical inspection reveals attack surface without network scanning

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to reduce IoT and AI risks
slides.slidecontent:
• Segment IoT devices onto isolated network zones
• Apply regular firmware updates and security patches
• Disable unused ports, protocols, and default credentials
• Monitor device behavior for anomalies indicating compromise
• Implement strong authentication and access controls
• Treat every connected device as a potential entry point

slides.subtitle: Personal practices for IoT and AI device safety
slides.slidecontent:
• Keep device firmware and software updated
• Use unique, strong passwords for each device and account
• Disable unnecessary features like unused wireless protocols
• Review and limit app permissions to essential functions only
• Secure home networks with strong encryption and passwords
• Stay informed about emerging threats and security practices

scripts.script: Welcome to the course on IoT and AI security. Before we dive into technical details, let's understand why this topic matters. We're living in a world where billions of everyday objects are connected to the internet. Your thermostat, doorbell, refrigerator, even your car. Increasingly, these devices use artificial intelligence to learn, adapt, and make autonomous decisions. While impressive, every new feature and connection creates opportunities for attackers. Cybercriminals no longer just target computers and servers, they're going after the smart devices in your home and workplace. The uncomfortable truth is that security is often treated as an afterthought when these products are designed. That's exactly what this course addresses.

Let's understand why IoT security creates unique challenges. When we talk about IoT devices, we're discussing physical objects connected to real networks. Thermostats, cameras, medical monitors, and industrial sensors, each representing a potential entry point for attackers. Adding AI makes things more complex because it can introduce behaviors that even developers didn't fully anticipate. What makes this serious is that these aren't just digital problems. Vulnerabilities can cause real, physical harm, whether that's a medical device malfunctioning or safety systems being compromised remotely. Many IoT devices never receive security updates, sitting on networks for years unpatched and exposed. This is the challenging landscape we must navigate.

Here's something that often surprises people about IoT security: much of the risk isn't digital at all, it's physical. Consider where these devices actually end up. Smart cameras in parking areas, sensors mounted in hallways, AI-enabled devices in reception areas, completely accessible to the public. Often there's no locked cabinet or protective housing preventing someone from interfering with them. If someone can physically reach a device, they can reboot it, tamper with it, or simply remove it. Many devices still have USB ports and reset buttons fully exposed, essentially creating an open invitation for attackers. Physical access is a critical security concern that's far too often ignored.

Remote IoT deployments present unique physical security challenges. Think about temperature sensors mounted on building exteriors or smart meters in parking structures. There's no security personnel, no surveillance cameras, often no physical barriers whatsoever. This creates serious problems because physical access can completely bypass software security controls. An attacker who can handle a device for just minutes can extract stored credentials, copy firmware, or install malicious hardware. The small form factor of many IoT devices makes them easy to steal or swap out without anyone noticing. Once they have the hardware, attackers have access to everything stored on it.

Why do physical security gaps persist in IoT deployments? Often it comes down to cost-cutting during installation. Proper protective enclosures, tamper-evident seals, and secure mounting hardware add expense that organizations try to avoid. Debug interfaces and service ports are left accessible to save technician time during maintenance. This creates serious vulnerabilities because brief physical access is often sufficient for an attacker to extract credentials or insert malicious components. When devices are stolen entirely, attackers gain access to local AI model weights and all stored data. The security measures do exist, but they're routinely skipped to reduce deployment costs.

IoT devices often reveal critical information through their external labeling. Model numbers, firmware versions, and manufacturer details printed on device housings provide attackers with valuable reconnaissance before they even attempt an attack. With this information, they can research known vulnerabilities, locate service documentation, and identify default configurations or backdoor accounts. This allows attackers to prepare targeted exploits based on physical inspection alone, without needing to perform network scanning that might trigger security alerts. It's essentially providing a roadmap for potential attacks, yet these informative labels remain standard practice across the industry.

Now let's discuss practical steps for reducing IoT and AI security risks. Start by implementing network segmentation, keeping IoT devices isolated from critical systems. Maintain regular firmware updates and security patches across all connected devices. Before deployment, disable unused ports, protocols, and change all default credentials. Implement continuous monitoring to detect anomalous device behavior that might indicate compromise. Strong authentication and access controls are essential for any device management interfaces. Most importantly, adopt the mindset that every connected device is a potential entry point into your network, not a trusted endpoint. This defensive approach significantly improves your overall security posture.

Let's conclude with essential security habits for managing IoT and AI devices in your personal and professional life. Keep all device firmware and software current with regular updates. Use unique, strong passwords for each device and associated account, avoiding password reuse. Disable unnecessary features like unused wireless protocols or remote access capabilities you don't need. When installing device applications, carefully review permissions and limit access to only essential functions. Secure your network infrastructure with strong encryption and authentication. Finally, stay curious and informed about emerging threats and security practices. Understanding how these technologies work positions you to better protect against evolving risks.

Who is this for: IT managers, network administrators, system administrators, operations managers, facility managers, security coordinators, technology managers, IoT device managers
Team or Dept this is for: IT, Operations, Facilities Management, Security, Technology
